runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the U.S. with respect to the net-of-tax wage
citations: Eissa and Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers
Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, taxes, and labor supply of single mothers
Keane (2011) - Labor supply and taxes: a survey of estimates
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely recognized in public finance as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) labor supply elasticities. The EITC literature (Eissa & Liebman 1996, Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) finds large participation responses to tax incentives, with implied elasticities often in the 0.5–1.0 range. Survey articles (e.g., Keane 2011, Chetty et al. 2012) place extensive-margin elasticities for this group notably above those for prime-age men (near zero) and married women (moderate). A central estimate around 0.7 reflects the clustering of credible quasi-experimental estimates, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.25 to 1.5 to account for variation across studies, time periods, and identification strategies.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the U.S. with respect to the net-of-tax wage", "point_estimate": 0.7, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.25, "p25": 0.5, "p50": 0.7, "p75": 1.0, "p95": 1.5}, "citations": ["Eissa and Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers", "Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, taxes, and labor supply of single mothers", "Keane (2011) - Labor supply and taxes: a survey of estimates"], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are widely recognized in public finance as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) labor supply elasticities. The EITC literature (Eissa & Liebman 1996, Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) finds large participation responses to tax incentives, with implied elasticities often in the 0.5\u20131.0 range. Survey articles (e.g., Keane 2011, Chetty et al. 2012) place extensive-margin elasticities for this group notably above those for prime-age men (near zero) and married women (moderate). A central estimate around 0.7 reflects the clustering of credible quasi-experimental estimates, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly 0.25 to 1.5 to account for variation across studies, time periods, and identification strategies."}

citations: Eissa and Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers
Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, taxes, and labor supply of single mothers

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely regarded as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) labor supply elasticities of any demographic group. Eissa and Liebman (1996) found substantial participation responses to the 1986 EITC expansion, and Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) estimated participation elasticities in the range of 0.7-1.1 for single mothers responding to tax and welfare policy changes in the 1990s. Surveys of the literature (e.g., Keane 2011, Chetty et al. 2013) generally place the extensive margin elasticity for this group in the 0.5-1.0 range, with some estimates higher. I center my belief at 0.7, reflecting a typical medium-run estimate, with meaningful right-tail uncertainty extending above 1.0 given variation across studies and identification strategies.

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are consistently found to have among the highest extensive-margin (participation) labor supply elasticities of any demographic group. The EITC literature (Eissa & Liebman 1996, Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) finds large participation responses to changes in after-tax wages, with estimates typically in the 0.5–1.0 range. Keane's survey and the broader public finance literature place the extensive margin elasticity for single mothers around 0.6–0.9. I center my estimate at 0.7, reflecting the clustering of credible quasi-experimental estimates, with meaningful uncertainty spanning roughly 0.3 to 1.2 to account for variation across studies, identification strategies, time periods, and the specific definition of net-of-tax wage used.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the U.S. with respect to the net-of-tax wage
citations: Eissa and Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers
Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, taxes, and labor supply of single mothers
Keane and Rogerson (2012) - Micro and macro labor supply elasticities survey
runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely recognized in the labor economics literature as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) elasticities of any demographic group. Eissa and Liebman (1996) found substantial participation responses to the 1986 EITC expansion, and Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) estimated participation elasticities in the range of 0.7-1.1 for single mothers responding to tax and transfer changes in the 1990s. The Keane and Rogerson survey and related work by Chetty et al. place extensive-margin elasticities for this group notably above the population average (which is often around 0.25-0.5). I center my estimate at 0.7, reflecting that most credible quasi-experimental estimates cluster in the 0.5-1.0 range, with some higher estimates when welfare reform effects are included. The wide uncertainty band (0.25 to 1.5) reflects variation across identification strategies, time periods, and whether the estimate captures pure tax-price effects or also accounts for correlated policy changes like welfare reform.
runs.rawResponse: {"interpretation": "Medium-run employment participation elasticity of single mothers in the U.S. with respect to the net-of-tax wage", "point_estimate": 0.7, "quantiles": {"p05": 0.25, "p25": 0.5, "p50": 0.7, "p75": 1.0, "p95": 1.5}, "citations": ["Eissa and Liebman (1996) - EITC expansion and labor supply of single mothers", "Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) - Welfare, taxes, and labor supply of single mothers", "Keane and Rogerson (2012) - Micro and macro labor supply elasticities survey"], "reasoning_summary": "Single mothers are widely recognized in the labor economics literature as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) elasticities of any demographic group. Eissa and Liebman (1996) found substantial participation responses to the 1986 EITC expansion, and Meyer and Rosenbaum (2001) estimated participation elasticities in the range of 0.7-1.1 for single mothers responding to tax and transfer changes in the 1990s. The Keane and Rogerson survey and related work by Chetty et al. place extensive-margin elasticities for this group notably above the population average (which is often around 0.25-0.5). I center my estimate at 0.7, reflecting that most credible quasi-experimental estimates cluster in the 0.5-1.0 range, with some higher estimates when welfare reform effects are included. The wide uncertainty band (0.25 to 1.5) reflects variation across identification strategies, time periods, and whether the estimate captures pure tax-price effects or also accounts for correlated policy changes like welfare reform."}

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely recognized in public finance as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) labor supply elasticities. The EITC literature (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) consistently finds large participation responses to tax changes, with elasticities often in the range of 0.5 to 1.0 or higher. These estimates reflect the fact that single mothers face high effective marginal tax rates from benefit phase-outs and have relatively elastic labor force entry/exit margins. My central estimate of 0.7 reflects a moderate reading of this literature, with uncertainty spanning from around 0.25 (if responses are attenuated or identification issues bias estimates up) to 1.5 (consistent with some of the larger estimates in the EITC and welfare reform literature).

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are among the most elastic groups on the extensive (participation) margin. The EITC literature (Eissa & Liebman 1996; Meyer & Rosenbaum 2001) finds substantial participation responses to changes in net-of-tax wages/income, with elasticities typically in the range of 0.5 to 1.2 on the extensive margin. Blundell and MaCurdy's survey and subsequent meta-analyses place the participation elasticity for this group notably higher than for married men or married women. My median estimate of 0.7 reflects the central tendency of these findings, with uncertainty spanning from about 0.25 (if responses are more muted or identification is less clean) up to 1.5 (reflecting some higher estimates in the literature, especially from policy reforms like welfare reform combined with EITC expansions).

runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are consistently found to have among the highest participation elasticities of any demographic group. runs.reasoningSummary: Single mothers are widely recognized in the labor economics literature as having among the highest extensive-margin (participation) elasticities of any demographic group. The wide interval (0.25 to 1.5) reflects genuine disagreement in the literature and sensitivity to methodology.
